<findings>

[relationship] [warning] Service-provider boundary with hench-agent-core still leaks upward responsibilities via the remaining callbacks, preventing a clean tools-only layer.

</findings>

<insights>

- Acts as the gateway between agent logic and concrete execution (`process/*`, `tools/*`).
- Partial coupling back to `hench` suggests boundaries could be clearer, though cohesion remains healthy.
- This layer exports numerous entrypoints (`process/index.ts`, `tools/index.ts`, `contracts.ts`), so define a thin compatibility shim to keep agent-core consumers from reaching into internal helpers such as `shared/glob.ts`.
- Seven imports back into `hench` reveal a dependency cycle; isolate bidirectional calls through explicit interfaces (e.g., an agent-to-tools adapter) to reduce tight coupling.
- Gateway modules (`exec-shell.ts`, `tools/dispatch.ts`) should maintain strict error normalization so upstream orchestration can remain transport-agnostic; consider adding conformance tests mirroring agent expectations.
- Tooling receives most of its traffic (56 incoming calls) from hench-agent-core yet issues a handful of calls back (6), meaning it behaves as a service provider that still pokes into orchestrator internals when it needs lifecycle info.
- Service-provider boundary with hench-agent-core still leaks upward responsibilities via the remaining callbacks, preventing a clean tools-only layer.
- [call graph] 121 internal calls, 6 outgoing, 56 incoming (cohesion: 0.95, coupling: 0.05)

</insights>
